title: Design a Disaster Recovery Architecture for SAP
description: RPO/RTO definitions for SAP (financial close, payroll, order processing), active-active vs active-passive, cross-region replication (storage vs DB vs app), failover automation, data consistency verification, GameDays/chaos engineering, cost analysis.

description.value:
Disaster Recovery (DR) for SAP systems is fundamentally different from DR for stateless web applications. SAP systems have state (in-memory data in HANA), long-running business processes (financial close can take 8 hours), and strict regulatory requirements (financial data must never be lost). The first step in any DR architecture is defining RPO and RTO per business process.

RPO (Recovery Point Objective): How much data loss is acceptable? For financial transactions, the answer is zero — you cannot lose a single posted invoice. For the financial close process, RPO = 0 (zero data loss). For transactional order processing, RPO = 5 seconds (a few orders might be re-entered by customers). For batch reporting, RPO = 1 hour (reports can be re-run after failover). These differences matter because achieving RPO=0 requires synchronous replication (expensive, adds latency), while RPO=5 seconds can use asynchronous replication with minimal latency impact.

RTO (Recovery Time Objective): How long can the system be down? The financial close process has RTO = 15 minutes if close is in progress (every minute of delay impacts SEC filings). Payroll processing has RTO = 4 hours (payroll can be delayed by a few hours if it's not payday). Standard order processing has RTO = 1 hour (customers can wait, but each hour costs $500K in lost orders). Development/test systems have RTO = 24 hours (no business impact). The RTO determines the failover approach: 15-minute RTO requires automated failover with pre-warmed standby; 1-hour RTO allows semi-automated failover with manual verification.

Combined RPO/RTO Matrix: The final DR requirements are a matrix of process vs. time-of-year. The financial close on the last day of the quarter has the strictest requirements (RPO=0, RTO=15min). Payroll on the day before payday has RPO=0, RTO=1hr. Regular operations on a Tuesday have RPO=5s, RTO=1hr. The DR architecture must support multiple tiers of protection that can be adjusted based on the business calendar.

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What is the cost of downtime per hour?" Financial close downtime costs $5M+/hour. Payroll downtime costs $500K/hour. Dev/test downtime costs $0. The RTO is directly proportional to the cost of downtime. The RPO is determined by the cost of data loss.

branches.condition: Financial close in progress — cost of downtime > $5M/hour
branches.path: RPO = 0, RTO = 15 minutes. Requires synchronous HANA System Replication with automated failover. No data loss tolerated. Pre-warmed standby in secondary region.

branches.condition: Order processing during business hours — cost of downtime $500K/hour
branches.path: RPO = 5 seconds, RTO = 1 hour. Asynchronous HANA replication. Semi-automated failover with manual approval gate. Acceptable to lose a few seconds of orders.

branches.condition: Batch processing or dev/test — no direct revenue impact
branches.path: RPO = 1 hour, RTO = 24 hours. Backup-based recovery. No real-time replication. Restore from latest backup. Cheapest option.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Do not give a single RPO/RTO number. The Staff+ answer provides a matrix: RPO/RTO per business process and per time period. Financial close on quarter-end has different requirements than a regular Tuesday. Show the interviewer you think in business impact, not raw technical capability.

variables.name: RPO=0 cost premium
variables.source: Synchronous HSR requires dedicated cross-region circuits

cp.monthlyPenalty: $45,000 RPO=0 sync replication: dedicated 10Gbps direct connect + 2x HANA license + automated failover tooling

ap.monthlyPenalty: $18,000 RPO=5s async replication: standard cross-region VPN + 1.5x HANA DR license + semi-automated failover

calculation.breakeven: At $500K/hour downtime cost, the 2.5x premium for RPO=0 pays for itself after 3 minutes of avoided downtime per month. For most enterprises, the financial close window alone justifies the premium.

description.value:
The fundamental architectural decision for SAP DR is whether to run an active-active configuration (both regions serving traffic) or active-passive (one region active, one region on standby). Each approach has tradeoffs that affect cost, complexity, and recovery time.

Active-Passive (Recommended for SAP): The primary region runs the full SAP stack (application servers + HANA database). The secondary region has a standby HANA database (with pre-loaded data) and pre-provisioned application servers that are stopped or running at minimal capacity. During failover: (1) HANA replication switches from primary to secondary (HANA System Replication with takeover). (2) Application servers in the secondary region are started. (3) DNS is updated to point to the secondary region. (4) Integration flows (RFC, IDoc) reconnect to the secondary region. Expected RTO: 15–30 minutes for Tier 1. Benefit: 50% cost of active-active (no need to run two full stacks). Challenge: the standby application servers must be patched and configured identically, which requires infrastructure-as-code and regular sync testing.

Active-Active (Not Recommended for SAP Core): Both regions run the full SAP stack and share the workload. This requires a distributed database (SAP HANA supports read-enabled secondary but not truly active-active writes) or application-level sharding. For SAP, true active-active writes to HANA is extremely complex because of SAP's locking model (enqueue server) and the need for global serialization of business transactions. SAP S/4HANA Cloud uses a multi-region active-active model, but it requires SAP-specific infrastructure (BTP, Cloud Foundry) that most on-premise customers don't have. Active-active can work for read-only workloads (reporting, analytics) with read replicas in the secondary region.

Warm Standby (Practical Compromise): A warm standby configuration runs the secondary HANA database as a replica (synchronous or asynchronous) with the application stack available but not running (stopped instances that can be started within 5 minutes). The warm standby is the most cost-effective option for Tier 2/3 processes. The tradeoff is that the secondary region cannot serve read traffic (unlike active-active), but the cost savings are significant: 30–40% of running a full active-active stack.

organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Start with active-passive, evolve toward active-active for read workloads only. Phase 1 (months 1-6): deploy active-passive with HANA System Replication and pre-provisioned standby. Phase 2 (months 7-12): enable read-only workload on the standby HANA for reporting and analytics — this converts passive DR into a cost-recovery asset. Phase 3 (year 2): evaluate active-active for specific non-SAP microservices that can run independently in both regions.

stakeholders.concern: Active-active for SAP requires distributed HANA which we have never operated. The operational risk is too high.
stakeholders.address: We agree. Active-active is not recommended for SAP HANA core. SAP's locking model (enqueue server) requires global serialization that active-active fundamentally breaks. Our active-active plan applies only to stateless or eventually consistent microservices (document management, notification service) where the operational risk is minimal.

stakeholders.concern: DR doubles our infrastructure cost. We need to justify the investment.
stakeholders.address: Active-passive costs 1.5x production (not 2x) because we scale down DR compute and use HANA DR licensing at 50%. By enabling read-only workloads on the standby in Phase 2, we offset 30% of the DR cost through analytics query offload. The net DR premium is 1.2x production — not 2x.

stakeholders.concern: Active-passive means we accept 15-30 minutes of downtime. Our board expects near-zero downtime.
stakeholders.address: 15-30 minute RTO meets the Tier 1 financial close requirement. For non-critical systems, RTO of 1-4 hours is acceptable. The cost to achieve sub-minute RTO (active-active with distributed HANA) is 5x higher and introduces data consistency risks that regulators would not accept. We recommend presenting the board with the RPO/RTO matrix and cost tradeoffs.

sections.title: Cross-Region Replication — Storage vs DB vs App

description.value:
Replication is the mechanism that keeps the DR region synchronized with the primary region. Choosing the right replication layer is critical — too low-level (storage replication) and you lose application consistency; too high-level (application replication) and you add latency and complexity.

Storage-Level Replication (Block/SAN): The storage system (SAN, AWS EBS, Azure Disk) replicates data blocks to the remote region. This is the most transparent to the application — SAP and HANA don't know replication is happening. However, storage replication has a major flaw: it replicates at the block level, which means the replicated data may be inconsistent if a write is in progress when the failover happens. HANA's in-memory state is also not captured by block replication. Storage replication is not recommended for HANA databases but can work for file shares (SAP transport directory, shared filesystem).

Database-Level Replication (HANA System Replication): This is the recommended approach for SAP HANA. HANA System Replication (HSR) works at the database level: the primary HANA instance continuously ships log entries to the secondary instance. The secondary applies the logs to maintain a synchronized copy. HSR supports synchronous mode (RPO=0) where the primary waits for the secondary to acknowledge before committing — this adds 1–5ms of latency. Asynchronous mode (RPO=5s) allows the primary to commit without waiting for the secondary. HSR also supports multi-target replication (one primary replicating to multiple secondaries) and takeover (the secondary becomes the primary with a single command).

Application-Level Replication (Async Event Distribution): For non-SAP systems or edge services, replication can be managed at the application layer via event-driven architecture. When an application processes a request, it publishes an event to Kafka. The DR region consumes the event and applies it to its local state. This provides the most flexibility (different applications can have different replication strategies) but requires each application to implement its own replication logic. This is appropriate for microservices edge services but not for the SAP core (too many synchronous side effects in BAdIs, user exits, and RFC calls).

cp.monthlyPenalty: $42,000 sync: standby HANA at 70% capacity ($28K) + dedicated cross-region circuit ($10K) + quarterly failover test ($4K/mo amortized)

ap.monthlyPenalty: $22,000 async: standby HANA at 50% capacity ($18K) + standard VPN ($2K) + less frequent testing ($2K/mo amortized)

calculation.breakeven: Sync replication costs 1.9x async but eliminates data loss. For financial close (12 hours/month), the sync premium of $20K/month is justified by avoiding even one data loss incident (potential $5M regulatory fine). For non-close periods, async is sufficient.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your active-passive DR costs 1.5x production but the standby does nothing until failover. Why not use the standby for read workloads to offset the cost?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "HANA standby cannot serve reads because it is in recovery mode." (technically incorrect — HANA supports read-enabled secondary since SPS 04)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "That is an excellent cost optimization question. SAP HANA actually supports read-enabled secondary since SPS 04 — the standby can serve read-only queries while replication is active. However, there is a critical catch: the read-enabled secondary serves data that is eventually consistent with the primary — typically 1-5 seconds behind depending on replication lag. This means we can offload reporting and analytics queries that tolerate 5-second staleness. The workloads we can redirect: (1) SAP BW/SAC reporting queries — they already have built-in data latency tolerance, (2) operational dashboards with 1-minute refresh intervals, (3) read-only copies of material master and customer data for lookup services. The workloads we CANNOT redirect: (1) financial close queries requiring real-time balances, (2) any write operations, (3) real-time inventory checks. The cost impact: by redirecting 30% of read traffic to the standby, we reduce primary HANA load by 30%, saving approximately $6,000/month in primary HANA capacity. This offsets 15% of the DR cost premium. The standby is no longer 'doing nothing' — it is a cost-recovery asset."
adversarialResponse.pattern: correct-and-optimize — accept the suggestion, provide accurate technical clarification (read-enabled secondary exists), give specific examples of what can and cannot be redirected, quantify the cost savings. The Staff+ signal is turning a cost center into a cost-recovery asset.

description.value:
Failover automation is the difference between a disaster recovery plan and a disaster recovery hope. Without automation, failover is a manual process that takes hours — and under stress, humans make mistakes. The goal is a fully automated failover that requires zero human intervention for Tier 1 processes.

Failover Runbook: The automated runbook defines the sequence of steps. Step 1: Detection (30s) — Health monitoring detects loss of quorum in the primary region. Check: HANA system replication status, application server health endpoints, load balancer health checks, and external dependency connectivity. Detection uses a quorum-based approach: at least 2 of 3 independent health monitors must agree that the primary is down before initiating failover. Step 2: Decision (10s) — The automation system checks if the secondary is healthy and has the latest data. If the replication lag exceeds the RPO threshold, the system alerts and pauses for manual decision. If the secondary is healthy, failover proceeds automatically. Step 3: Execution (5–15min) — HANA takeover (secondary becomes primary), start application servers in DR region, reroute network traffic (DNS update or load balancer reconfiguration), restart integration flows (RFC destinations, IDoc receivers). Step 4: Verification (5min) — Automated smoke tests check: can users log in? Can we post a transaction? Is SAP HANA responding? Is the enqueue server healthy? The verification step confirms that the DR region is fully operational.

Gated Failover: Not all failovers should be automatic. The failover gate determines automation level based on current RPO/RTO needs. During the financial close period, failover is fully automatic. During regular operations, failover requires a human approval (one-click approve). During known maintenance windows, failover is suspended. The gate is controlled by an operations calendar that the DR automation reads.

Automated Fallback: After the primary region is restored, the system must fail back. Fallback is more complex than failover because data created in the DR region must be replicated back to the restored primary. The fallback process: (1) Establish reverse replication from DR to restored primary. (2) Wait for replication lag to reach zero. (3) Execute controlled switchover (not a failover — a planned operation). (4) Verify restored primary is healthy. (5) Resume normal operations. The entire fallback should be practiced quarterly to ensure the team knows the process.

Process</text><text class="fo-sub" x="180" y="255">1. Establish reverse replication</text><text class="fo-sub" x="180" y="271">2. Wait for lag to reach zero</text><text class="fo-sub" x="180" y="287">3. Controlled switchover</text><text class="fo-sub" x="180" y="303">4. Verify primary health</text><text class="fo-sub" x="180" y="319">5. Resume normal ops</text><rect class="fo-box" x="355" y="215" width="310" height="120"/><text class="fo-lbl" x="510" y="235">Runbook Automation Tools</text><text class="fo-sub" x="510" y="255">HANA takeover: SAP HANA cockpit</text><text class="fo-sub" x="510" y="271">App start: Terraform / Ansible</text><text class="fo-sub" x="510" y="287">Network: DNS / Global LB update</text><text class="fo-sub" x="510" y="303">Integration: RFC destination swap</text><text class="fo-sub" x="510" y="319">Verification: Automated smoke tests</text></svg>

Failover is not complete until data consistency is verified. A failover that succeeds technically but produces inconsistent data is worse than no failover — it gives a false sense of recovery while corrupting business processes. SAP systems have complex consistency requirements that go beyond database-level checks.

HANA Consistency Checks: After a HANA takeover, automated checks run against the database: (1) Transaction log consistency — verify that the last log entry on the secondary matches the commit log on the primary (checksum validation). (2) Index consistency — verify that HANA column store indexes are valid and not corrupted. (3) Replication consistency — compare row counts on critical tables (ACDOCA, BKPF, MSEG) between the primary (if accessible) and secondary. (4) referential integrity — check that foreign key relationships are intact (e.g., every line item has a header). These checks run in 2–5 minutes and produce a consistency report.

Application-Layer Consistency: Database-level consistency does not guarantee application-layer consistency. For SAP, we must verify: (1) IDoc processing state — are there in-flight IDocs that were committed on the primary but not yet processed by the secondary? The IDoc status table (EDIDC/EDID4) must be checked for consistency. (2) Batch job state — are there batch jobs that were running on the primary and must be restarted? The TBTCO/TBTCP tables are checked. (3) Lock state — the enqueue server lock table must be consistent; all locks held by failed application servers must be released. (4) RFC queue state — qRFC queues must be checked for consistency; transactions that were in-flight during the failover may need to be re-processed.

Business-Layer Verification: The final verification is at the business level. The verification system posts a test financial transaction (a $1 invoice to a test vendor), processes it through the full flow (FI → CO → MM integration), and verifies the result. This end-to-end smoke test catches issues that no technical check can: missing customizing, incorrect integration mappings, or permission problems. If the test transaction fails, the failover is considered incomplete, and the operations team is alerted.

decisionTree.question: After a HANA takeover, what consistency verification depth is appropriate for your DR plan?

branches.condition: Financial close period — zero tolerance for data loss
branches.action: Run all three verification layers (HANA + Application + Business). The business-layer test posts and reconciles a $1 invoice through FI/CO. Do not cut over to DR until all three layers pass. Expected time: 8–12 minutes.
branches.tradeoff: Maximum safety but longest verification window; may exceed 15-minute RTO if verification reveals issues requiring manual resolution.

branches.action: Run HANA checks (transaction log, index, row counts) + application-layer checks (IDoc state, batch jobs, locks). Skip business-layer smoke test unless triggered manually. Expected time: 3–5 minutes.
branches.tradeoff: Good safety with minimal delay; risk that application-layer verification misses semantic data corruption that only a business transaction would catch.

branches.condition: Read-only DR drill or non-production system
branches.action: Run only HANA-level checks: transaction log consistency and index validation. Skip row-count comparison and all application-layer checks. Expected time: 1–2 minutes.
branches.tradeoff: Fastest verification; acceptable only when the system will not serve production write traffic.

description.value:
GameDays (also called Chaos Engineering) are the only way to verify that your DR architecture actually works. Simulated disasters are practiced regularly so that when a real disaster happens, the team is prepared, the automation works, and the RTO/RPO targets are met. For SAP systems, GameDays must be carefully scoped because disrupting a financial system during business hours is unacceptable.

GameDay Frequency: Three tiers of GameDays. Full DR Test (quarterly, during a planned maintenance window) — simulate a complete region failure. All traffic is failed over to the DR region. The system runs on DR for 4–8 hours. All business processes are tested. Then failback is executed. This is the most expensive test but the only one that truly validates readiness. Component Failure Test (monthly, during low-traffic hours) — fail a single component: take down one application server, failover HANA to secondary, simulate an RFC connection failure. Each component is tested quarterly. Integration Test (weekly, automated) — verify that DR connectivity is working: DR application servers can connect to DR HANA, RFC destinations point to DR systems, IDoc processing works in DR. Automated scripts run these tests without human intervention.

Chaos Scenarios for SAP: Specific scenarios that must be tested. (1) HANA node failure — kill the primary HANA node, verify automatic takeover. (2) Full region failure — simulate an AWS/Azure region outage, trigger failover. (3) Network partition — simulate connectivity loss between primary and secondary, verify behavior (synchronous replication should pause, not corrupt data). (4) Data corruption — simulate a corrupted table in HANA, verify that the secondary has uncorrupted data. (5) Application server failure — kill all application server processes, verify that the load balancer redirects traffic to remaining servers. (6) RFC destination failure — drop the RFC connection to a key integration, verify queuing and retry behavior.

Post-GameDay Review: Every GameDay produces an After-Action Report (AAR) with: what was tested, what happened (expected vs actual), issues found, time to recover, and action items. Action items are assigned, tracked, and must be resolved before the next GameDay. A common finding after the first GameDay: the DR runbook is outdated or the automation has a bug that only manifests during actual failover. This is exactly why GameDays are essential — they find the gaps that documentation audits miss.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Weekly automated integration tests seem excessive for DR. Our system has been stable for two years with quarterly full DR tests only. What is the real value of weekly tests?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Best practices say you should run weekly tests." (authoritative without justification — does not address cost-benefit)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "The value is not in finding failures — it is in ensuring that the DR automation actually works when you need it. Our quarterly full DR tests always pass because the team knows they are coming. The weekly integration tests exist specifically to catch configuration drift between tests. In our experience, 3–4 times per year, a weekly test catches something: a recently patched application server that lost its DR HANA connection, a firewall rule change that blocked RFC traffic, or an expired certificate on the DR load balancer. Each caught issue would have caused a full failover to fail. The cost of the weekly test is negligible (fully automated, 5 minutes, no human involvement). The cost of a failed full DR test is a $50K+ post-mortem and a compliance finding."
adversarialResponse.pattern: reframe-the-question — shift from frequency justification to defect-detection value. Provide specific real-world examples of configuration drift that weekly tests catch. Quantify the cost of a failed full DR test versus the cost of weekly testing.

organizationalDimension.teamStructure: The GameDay program is owned by the SRE team, but each GameDay requires a Business Process Owner (BPO) from the SAP functional team to define acceptance criteria for business-level verification. The BPO decides which SAP transactions must pass and what constitutes acceptable data consistency.
organizationalRisks: SRE teams may run technical test scenarios (HANA failover, network partition) without validating business processes, creating false confidence that DR is working when business transactions would actually fail.
Business teams may resist GameDays during financial close periods, leading to scheduling gaps that leave the most critical time window untested.
organizationalDimension.staffPlusAdvice: Establish a GameDay calendar at the beginning of each fiscal year that blocks known windows for all three test tiers. Tie each GameDay outcome to an executive dashboard that shows RTO/RPO attainment trends. When a GameDay fails, the root-cause analysis is shared at the monthly operations review — not to assign blame, but to build organizational muscle for incident response. The SRE team should rotate the GameDay lead role quarterly so that at least four engineers are competent to run a full DR test.

description.value:
Disaster Recovery is expensive. Running a fully redundant infrastructure in a second region can double your infrastructure costs. In FAANG system design interviews, the cost analysis is where Staff+ engineers distinguish themselves — they show that they understand the financial implications of their architecture and can optimize costs without sacrificing reliability.

Cost Components: The major cost drivers for SAP DR are: (1) HANA Licensing — SAP HANA is licensed per GB of memory. If you have a 2TB HANA instance in the primary, the secondary also needs 2TB (you can use HANA's storage replication to run the secondary on lower-cost hardware, but you still need enough memory to load the full dataset). HANA DR licensing is typically 50% of production license cost. (2) Compute — Application server instances, load balancers, and supporting infrastructure. For active-passive, the DR compute can be stopped or scaled down to 20% of production capacity (enough to run verification tests). For active-active, compute is at 100% in both regions. (3) Network — Cross-region data transfer costs. Synchronous HANA replication requires high-bandwidth, low-latency connections (AWS Direct Connect or Azure ExpressRoute at 10Gbps+). This can cost $10K–$50K/month depending on distance. (4) Storage — HANA data volumes, backups, and transaction logs. DR storage is a mirror of primary storage.

Cost Optimization Strategies: (1) Scale-Down DR — Run the DR HANA on a smaller instance type with sufficient memory for the dataset but fewer CPUs. SAP's HANA System Replication supports heterogeneous scale-down targets. Cost reduction: 30–40%. (2) Preemptible/Spot DR Instances — For application servers in the DR region (not the HANA database), use spot/preemptible instances that can be replaced if capacity is reclaimed. Cost reduction: 60–70% on compute. (3) Cross-Region vs. Cross-AZ — For Tier 2/3 processes, consider cross-AZ (within the same region) instead of cross-region. Cross-AZ has lower latency, lower network cost, and simpler failover. Cost reduction: 50% vs cross-region. (4) Shared DR — If you have multiple SAP environments (DEV, QA, PROD), the DR infrastructure can be shared across non-production environments during non-critical periods.

Analysis</text><text class="co-sub" x="345" y="308">Always include cost discussion in your DR design — it shows you understand tradeoffs</text><text class="co-sub" x="345" y="325">FAANG cares about TCO, not just technical elegance. Optimize for cost without sacrificing RPO/RTO.</text></svg>

details: Staff+ Insight: Disaster Recovery for SAP is a favorite system design question because it combines infrastructure knowledge (replication, failover), business context (RPO/RTO per process), and cost awareness. The key insight: DR is not one-size-fits-all. Different business processes have different RPO/RTO requirements, and the architecture should tier the protection level. Most candidates design a single DR approach. Staff+ candidates design a tiered DR architecture with calendar-aware failover automation and a clear cost-benefit analysis.
